revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * [[Yayoi burial practices]] * [[Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).

revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).

These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * [[Yayoi burial practices]] * [[Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]

revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * [[Yayoi burial practices]] * [[Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).

revision.comment: 画像追加。
revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani Graves-2, funkyu.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[西谷墳墓群|西谷2号墓]]（島根県出雲市）}}]] '''四隅突出型墳丘墓'''（よすみとっしゅつがたふんきゅうぼ）は、[[弥生時代]]中期後葉から弥生時代終末期ごろに、おもに[[中国山地|中国山間部]]・[[山陰地方]]・[[北陸地方]]で採用された墓制で、方形墳丘墓の四隅がヒトデのように飛び出した特異な形の[[弥生墳丘墓|墳丘墓]]である。
== 概要 == [[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|青木4号墓（島根県出雲市）}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[杉谷古墳群|杉谷4号墓]]（富山県富山市）}}]] 四隅突出型墳丘墓は、1968年に順庵原1号（[[島根県]][[邑南町]]）で最初に確認された。北陸地方では1974年に杉谷4号墓（[[富山県]][[富山市]]）が確認されたのが最初である。100基余りが知られる。
[[三次市|三次盆地]]や[[出雲平野]]のものが弥生時代中期後葉で、最も古い例である。その後、後期前葉に[[伯耆国|伯耆]]・[[因幡国|因幡]]（[[鳥取県]]）、後期後葉に[[出雲国|出雲]]（島根県東部）で展開した。北陸にも後期後半以降広まり、[[越前国|越前]]（[[福井県]]東部）・[[加賀国|加賀]]（[[石川県]]南部）・[[越中国|越中]]（富山県）で発見されている。
起源としては、かつては[[朝鮮半島]]起源説{{sfn|山本|1975}}、[[方形周溝墓]]起源説{{sfn|小野山|1975}}なども唱えられたが、貼石方形墓起源説{{sfn|川原|1978}}が定説となっている。
墳丘墓側面に貼石をめぐらし、規模は後の前期古墳の規模に近づくなど、[[古墳時代]]以前の墓制ではもっとも土木技術が駆使されており、[[日本海]]沿岸という広域で墓制の共有が行われていた。ただし、山陰と北陸の中間地である北近畿では、四隅突出型墳丘墓は1基も見つかっていない。
== 中国山間部の四隅突出型墳丘墓 == 前述のとおり、備後北部の三次盆地で出現期の四隅突出型墳丘墓が多数見つかっている。陣山墳墓群（広島県三次市）では未だ墳墓のつくり方が定型化していない様相が確認され{{sfn|落田|1996}}、これらが最古の四隅突出型墳丘墓の一例とみられる。備後北部以外でも、[[石見国|石見]]の順庵原1号墓（島根県邑南町）、[[美作国|美作]]の竹田8号墓（[[岡山県]][[鏡野町]]）などが中国山間部の四隅突出型墳丘墓として理解される。
この地域では弥生時代中期後半以降、後期前葉まで四隅突出型墳丘墓が造営されるが、その後は途絶える。しかし、四隅突出型墳丘墓の時代としては最終末にあたる弥生古墳移行期に、隅の突出した前方後方形墳墓である[[矢谷墳丘墓]]（広島県三次市）が現れる{{sfn|松本|2003}}。
このほか中国山間部ではないが、[[播磨国|播磨]]（[[兵庫県]]南部）にも四隅突出型墳丘墓の可能性がある墳墓が発見されている（周遍寺山1号墓、船木南山古墳）。 <gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[矢谷墳丘墓]]（広島県三次市） </gallery>
== 山陰の四隅突出型墳丘墓 == [[File:Nishidani Graves-3, funkyu.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[西谷墳墓群|西谷3号墓]]（島根県出雲市）}}]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[妻木晩田遺跡]]（[[鳥取県]][[西伯郡]][[大山町]]）洞ノ原地区墳丘墓}}]] [[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[大城墳丘墓]]（島根県[[隠岐の島町]]）}}{{small|[[隠岐島]]では唯一の四隅突出型墳丘墓。}}]]
かつては四隅突出型墳丘墓が中国山間部で一元的に誕生し日本海沿岸部に伝播したと考えられていたが、島根県出雲市[[青木遺跡 (出雲市)|青木遺跡]]で出現期の様相をもった四隅突出型墳丘墓（青木4号墓）が発見され、出雲地域にも古い時期から四隅突出型墳丘墓が造られていたことが判明した。
弥生時代後期前葉から中葉には伯耆・因幡で四隅突出型墳丘墓が盛行する。この時代の代表的なものとして、[[妻木晩田遺跡]]（鳥取県[[大山町]]）の洞ノ原墳墓群・仙谷墳墓群や、阿弥大寺墳墓群（鳥取県[[倉吉市]]）、西桂見墳丘墓（鳥取県[[鳥取市]]）などがある。
弥生後期後葉から末葉においては、出雲西部では島根県出雲市の[[西谷墳墓群]]（西谷2号墓・3号墓・4号墓・9号墓）、出雲東部では島根県安来市の[[仲仙寺古墳群]]（仲仙寺9号墓・10号墓）、宮山Ⅳ号墓などが代表的な墳丘墓であり、この時期には出雲の西と東に大きな政治勢力が形成されたものと考えられる{{sfn|松本|2006}}。これらの墳丘墓は限られた丘陵などに累代的に造営された。また、この時期には隠岐で大城墳丘墓（島根県隠岐の島町）が造営されており、墓制の面から海を越えた交流が見いだせる。
西谷3号墓の埋葬施設はほぼ同時期の吉備の墳丘墓である[[楯築遺跡|楯築墳丘墓]]（岡山県[[倉敷市]]）のそれと同じような構造の木槨墓である。また、埋葬後の儀礼に用いた[[土器]]の中に北陸系の器台・高杯や吉備の[[特殊器台・特殊壺]]などが大量に混入していた{{sfn|渡辺|2018}}。
古墳時代になると、出雲平野では繁栄が止まり大型の古墳はしばらく作られない。一方の出雲東部では、古墳時代前期に全国的にも抜きん出た大型[[方墳]]（大成古墳、[[造山古墳 (安来市)|造山古墳]]ほか）が造営される。これらが四隅突出型墳丘墓の延長線上に築かれたものであるとする研究がある{{sfn|岩本|2017}}。
== 北陸の四隅突出型墳丘墓 == 北陸の四隅突出型墳丘墓は、弥生時代後期後葉に初めて登場する。
北陸の四隅突出型墳丘墓は、配石構造を欠く点において山陰の四隅突出型墳丘墓と様相を異にしている。このように、在地の墓制と外来の墓制（四隅突出型墳丘墓）の双方の影響下に成立したものであることから「北陸型四隅突出型墳丘墓」の名称を用いられることがある{{sfn|古川|1993}}。
西谷3号墓とほぼ同時期にあたる小羽山30号墓（福井県[[福井市]]）は山陰の影響を直接受けたとみられ、墳丘形態、副葬品、赤色顔料の付着した石杵など共通点が多い。このあとの時期については、継続的に山陰のものから影響を受けていたという説と北陸で独自に展開したという説がある。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *{{cite journal|和書|author=岩本崇|year=2017|title=再生された四隅突出型墳丘墓|journal=考古学研究|volume=64|number=1|ref={{sfnref|岩本|2017}}}} *{{cite journal|和書|author=落田正弘|year=1996|title=三次市陣山墳墓群について|journal=芸備|volume=25|ref={{sfnref|落田|1996}}}} *{{cite book|和書|author=小野山節|year=1975|title=古代史発掘6 古墳と王朝の歩み|publisher=講談社|ref={{sfnref|小野山|1975}}}} *{{cite journal|和書|author=川原和人|year=1978|title=島根県における発生期古墳：とくに四隅突出型方墳について|journal=古文化談叢|volume=4|ref={{sfnref|川原|1978}}}} *{{cite journal|和書|author=古川登|year=1993|title=北陸地方の四隅突出型墳丘墓について|journal=島根考古学会誌|volume=10|ref={{sfnref|古川|1993}}}} *{{cite book|和書|author=松本岩雄|year=2003|chapter出雲の四隅突出型墓|title=宮山古墳群の研究|publisher=島根県教育委員会|ref={{sfnref|松本|2003}}}} *{{cite book|和書|author=松本岩雄|year=2006|chapter=考古資料からみた弥生・古墳時代の北陸と山陰|editor=上原真人他|title=列島の古代史1 古代史の舞台|publisher=岩波書店|ref={{sfnref|松本|2006}}}} *{{cite journal|和書|author=山本清|year=1975|title=出雲の四隅突出型方墳|journal=日本のなかの朝鮮文化|volume=28|ref={{sfnref|山本|1975}}}} *{{cite book|和書|author=渡辺貞幸|year=2018|title=出雲王と四隅突出型墳丘墓 西谷墳墓群|publisher=新泉社|ref={{sfnref|渡辺|2018}}}}
== 関連項目 == *[[弥生時代の墓制]] *[[弥生墳丘墓]] *[[古墳GALのコフィー]]（この墳墓をモチーフにしたキャラクター「ダニエル」が登場する。）
{{DEFAULTSORT:よすみとつしゆつかたふんきゆうほ}} {{Normdaten}} [[category:弥生時代]] [[Category:日本の墳丘墓]] {{japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Fix the O]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * [[Yayoi burial practices]] * [[Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).
[[Category:Yayoi period]] [[Category:Burial mounds in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yosumi Tosshutsu funkyūbo}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yosumi Tosshutsu funkyūbo}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yosumi Tosshutsu funkyūbo}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]]

== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.comment: /* Related Topics */
revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制}} * [[Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]

In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>

== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.comment: /* Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains */
revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制}} * [[Yayoi tumulus burials|Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style). [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]

Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).

Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}}]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}}]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}}]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制}} * [[Yayoi tumulus burials|Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).
