Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}]]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}]]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}]]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}]]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制}} * [[Yayoi tumulus burials|Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style). <gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]

However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}]]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}]]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)}]]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)}]]] The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.}]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period}} * [[Yayoi tumulus burials|Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Kofun GAL no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style). mounds]][[Category:前方後方墳]] [[Category:前方後方墳]][[sn:Protruded-corner square mounds]][[Category:前方後方墳]] [[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] {{kofun navbar}}

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]]

== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)}}{{small|The only four-corner protrusion burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored four-corner protrusion burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a four-corner protrusion burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>

Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style four-corner protrusion burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>

In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).

These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.

revision.comment: /* X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region */
revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
<nowiki>[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the </nowiki>{{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]

== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.

[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds aqqeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) aqqeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]

== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Aqqearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Tumulus cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped burial mound at the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi remains|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}}, {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|ja|大山町}}, Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mounds''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial Mounds'') are a distinctive type of burial mound primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the {{ill|Chugoku Mountains|ja|中国山地}}, the {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped burial mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound ({{ill|Onan, Shimane|ja|邑南町}}, Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Burial Mound (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds aqqeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Burial Mound Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Burial Mound (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Burial Mound (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Burial Mound (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) aqqeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyogo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Burial Mound and Funakiminamiyama Burial Mound, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Burial Mound (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped burial mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Burial Mound (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the {{ill|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement|ja|妻木晩田遺跡}} (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Burial Mound Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Burial Mound (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the {{ill|Nishidani Burial Mound Group|ja|西谷墳墓群}} (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the {{ill|Nakazenji Burial Mound Group|ja|仲仙寺古墳群}} (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Burial Mound shares similarities with the {{ill|Tatezuka Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mounds in the Hokuriku Region ==
Aqqearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped burial mounds."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Burial Mound (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools.
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period}} * [[Yayoi tumulus burials|Yayoi burial mounds]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this burial mound style).
