revision.comment: Bot: update category link → Yayoi burial mounds
revision.text:
{{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>

[[sn:X-shaped Burial Mound]] [[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] [[Category:Yayoi burial mounds]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Kofun template and pages that transclude it
revision.text:
{{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島}}.]]

'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains ==
Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島}}.]]

'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>

[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|qq=|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|qq=|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]]
[[Category:San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns]] <!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四隅突出型墳丘墓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11420180]] Wikidata name: four corner projections type grave mound [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment |1= took a bit of liberty with the title because it’s really clunky otherwise.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:09, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Nishidani_Graves-2,_funkyu.jpg|thumb|[[Nishidani Kofun cluster]]]] [[File:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|thumb|250px|Restored X-shaped Burial Mound at the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi remains]], [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen, Tottori]], Tottori Prefecture.]]
'''X-shaped Burial Mound''' (四隅突出型墳丘墓, ''Yosumi Tosshutsugata Funkyūbo, lit Four-Corner Protrusion Burial mounds'') are a distinctive type of Kofun primarily adopted from the late Middle Yayoi period to the terminal Yayoi period. These mounds, characterized by starfish-like protrusions at their four corners, were used mainly in the [[:en:Chūgoku Mountains|Chugoku Mountains]], the [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], and the Hokuriku Region.
The ''yosumi-tosshutsugata'' style is unique to the late middle Yayoi period and is most prevalent in western Japan in areas influenced by the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo culture]]. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book|last1=Isomura|first1=Yukio|last2=Sakai|first2=Hideya|title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia|date=2012|publisher=学生社|isbn=43pp750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref>[[:en:Yadani_Kofun|Wikipedia - Yadani Kofun]]</ref>
== Overview ==
[[File:Aoki Site (Izumo), funkyubo.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo, Shimane Prefecture)]] [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-4, funkyu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama, Toyama Prefecture)]]The first example of a X-shaped Burial Mound was identified in 1968 at Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun ([[:en:Ōnan, Shimane|Onan, Shimane]], Shimane Prefecture). In the Hokuriku Region, the first recorded instance was Sugitani No. 4 Kofun (Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture) in 1974. Over 100 such mounds have since been documented.
These mounds appeared first in the {{ill|Miyoshi Basin|ja|三次盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Basin|1=Miyoshi Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356056}} and {{ill|Izumo Plain|ja|出雲平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Plain|1=Izumo Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395890}} during the late Middle Yayoi period, representing the earliest examples. Their use then spread to the Hōki and Inaba regions (Tottori Prefecture) in the early Late Yayoi period and to western and eastern Izumo during the late Yayoi period. By the mid-to-late Late Yayoi period, they had extended to the Hokuriku Region, including Echizen (eastern Fukui Prefecture), Kaga (southern Ishikawa Prefecture), and Etchū (Toyama Prefecture).
The origin of this burial style was once attributed to the Korean Peninsula, square-ditch tombs, or square stone-covered tombs, but the latter hypothesis has become the prevailing theory.<ref>{{sfn|Kawahara|1978}}</ref>
These mounds incorporated stone facings and resembled early kofun in scale, showcasing advanced civil engineering for pre-kofun burial practices. Despite being distributed across the Japan Sea coast, no examples have been found in the North Kinki region, situated between the San'in and Hokuriku regions.
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Chugoku Mountains == [[File:Distribution_of_Yosumi_tombs.png|thumb]] Numerous early examples have been identified in northern Bitchū Province, particularly in the Miyoshi Basin. At the Jin'yama Kofun Group (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture), variations in mound construction indicate that their design had not yet been standardized.<ref>{{sfn|Ochida|1996}}</ref> Other notable examples include Jun'anbara No. 1 Kofun (Onan, Shimane Prefecture) and Takeda No. 8 Kofun (Kagamino, Okayama Prefecture).
The practice of building such mounds in the Chugoku Mountains ceased by the early Late Yayoi period. However, the transitional Yadani Kofun (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) appeared during the terminal Yayoi period, showcasing corner protrusions resembling a proto-{{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2003}}</ref>
Examples in Harima (southern Hyōgo Prefecture), such as Shuuhenjiyama No. 1 Kofun and Funakiminamiyama Kofun, suggest a possible extension of this burial style into the region.
<gallery> Yadani Funkyu-bo, ortho.jpg|[[Yadani Kofun]] (Miyoshi, Hiroshima Prefecture) </gallery>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the San'in Region ==
[[File:Oshiro Funkyu-bo zenkei.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Ōshiro Kofun (Okinoshima Town, Shimane Prefecture)The only X-shaped Burial Mound on the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 隠岐諸島|1=Oki Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q483146}}.]]
Originally thought to have emerged in the Chugoku Mountains and spread to the Japan Sea coast, early examples like Aoki No. 4 Kofun (Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture) suggest that the Izumo region independently adopted this burial style from an early stage.
During the early Late Yayoi period, the Hōki and Inaba regions saw a proliferation of these mounds. Representative examples include the [[:en:Mukibanda Yayoi remains|Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement]] (Daisen, Tottori Prefecture), Amidaiji Kofun Group (Kurayoshi, Tottori Prefecture), and Nishitsuchimi Kofun (Tottori, Tottori Prefecture).
In the late Yayoi period, prominent examples in western Izumo include the [[:en:Nishidani Tumulus cluster|Nishidani Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 2, 3, 4, and 9), while eastern Izumo boasts the [[:en:Chūsenji Kofun Cluster|Nakazenji Kofun Group]] (Tombs No. 9 and 10) and Miyayama Tomb IV. These mounds indicate the emergence of significant political entities in both western and eastern Izumo.<ref>{{sfn|Matsumoto|2006}}</ref>
The Nishidani No. 3 Kofun shares similarities with the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] (Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture) in terms of burial chamber construction and grave goods.<ref>{{sfn|Watanabe|2018}}</ref>
== X-shaped Burial Mound in the Hokuriku Region ==
Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|version=103455726|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]]
[[Category:San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns]] <!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->
}}

Appearing in the late Yayoi period, Hokuriku examples lack stone facings seen in the San'in Region. Their hybrid characteristics suggest influences from both local and external burial practices, leading to the term "Hokuriku-style X-shaped Burial Mound."<ref>{{sfn|Furukawa|1993}}</ref>
One notable example, the Obayama No. 30 Kofun (Fukui City, Fukui Prefecture), shows direct influence from the San'in Region, with similarities in mound shape and grave goods, including red-painted stone tools. == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi burial practices|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} * [[Yayoi mounded tombs|Yayoi Kofuns]] * {{ill|Cofun Gal no Coffy|ja|古墳GALのコフィー|1=Cofun Gal no Coffy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|13=User:Immanuelle/Cofun Gal no Coffy|qq=}} (features the character "Daniel" based on this Kofun style).
